$comment: Master data file for the Carrigtwohill Historical Places map. See EDITING_GUIDE.md for a non-developer reference. Text in `blocks` of type `paragraph` is rendered verbatim — do not reformat historian prose.

places.name: Leamlara House
places.preview: Seat of the Coppinger family, set in wooded demesne north-east of Carrigtwohill. A site of continuous occupation with a complex architectural history.
places.lead: Seat of the Coppinger family for several centuries, Leamlara House stood at the centre of a substantial demesne.

blocks.text: The lands of Leamlara came into the possession of the Coppinger family in the seventeenth century, following a period of considerable upheaval in the region. The family, of Hiberno-Norse origin and long established in Cork, remained Catholic throughout the penal period and retained the estate through various legal arrangements.
The original house on the site was a fortified dwelling, successive rebuildings masking but not entirely effacing its earlier fabric.

The main block as it stood in the nineteenth century was a two-storey Georgian house of seven bays, with a central pedimented entrance and low wings extending to either side. A walled garden to the south, and extensive plantings of beech and oak to the north, framed the approach.

image.alt: Drawing of Leamlara House and grounds
image.caption: The demesne at Leamlara, showing walled garden and approach

blocks.text: The principal reception rooms were ranged along the south front, with the drawing room, dining room and library opening one into the next. A fine plaster ceiling in the drawing room survived into the twentieth century.
The surrounding demesne included a deer park, fish pond, and an avenue of limes planted in the eighteenth century. Outlying farms were worked by tenants under the usual arrangements of the period.

Like many Irish country houses, Leamlara entered a period of contraction in the early twentieth century. The house was reduced in size, and the demesne eventually broken up. The site remains of considerable interest to the local community.

There was never a finer avenue of limes in the district, and the loss of them was felt by every man and woman who had walked beneath their shade.
— Local recollection, recorded 1962

sources.author: Bence-Jones, M.
sources.title: A Guide to Irish Country Houses

sources.author: Coppinger, W. A.
sources.title: History of the Coppingers of Cork

sources: Carrigtwohill Historical Society archive, file LEA/1–12

places.name: Ballyannan Castle
places.preview: About two miles from Midleton stands the ruins of a well-known castle...

images.caption: Ballyannan Castle (placeholder image; source: Carrigtwohill Historical Society)

blocks.text: Ballyannan Castle was a tower house of the Barry family, built in the late fifteenth or early sixteenth century on the northern margin of what is now Ballyannan Wood. It commanded views over the inlet and the approach from the east.
By the middle of the seventeenth century the castle had been abandoned and reduced to a ruinous state. Today only fragments of the masonry remain, partly obscured by tree cover.

sources: Nora O Mahony of Cork Road Midleton, as told to her by her father in 1936 — Dúchas / Schools' Collection: https://www.duchas.ie/en/cbes/4921918/4910884/5166111

places.name: St. Mary's Parish Church, Carrigtwohill
places.preview: The Catholic parish church on the main street of Carrigtwohill, successor to a medieval foundation.

blocks.text: The present parish church stands in succession to earlier ecclesiastical sites in Carrigtwohill, of which traces survive in the townland name and in scattered references in the medieval record. The current building dates largely from the nineteenth century, with subsequent restorations.
